coredata.dc:title: Spermine induced in-situ synthesis of polychromatic carbon nanodots towards smartphone-readable ratiometric fluorescence sensing of multiple catecholamines
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jiajing
An, Jia
Han, Yaqin
Fang, Junan
Liu, Yufei
coredata.dc:description: Abnormal levels of catecholamines can indicate underlying health issues, such as Parkinson's disease, hypertension, and pheochromocytoma. However, it is difficult to accurately differentiate multiple catecholamines using traditional detection methods due to their highly similar structures. Herein, we proposed a carbon-based fluorescent probe with adaptability named spermine that can react with multiple catecholamines to generate polymer carbon dots with distinct fluorescence colors, enabling visual differentiation and quantitative detection of epinephrine, norepinephrine and levodopa. Spermine does not requires synthesis and exhibits excellent biocompatibility, as it naturally exists in bacteria and most animal cells. Additionally, ratiometric fluorescent probes with high sensitivity was constructed to eliminate nonspecific factors in the sample detection process. Furthermore, due to the simplicity of the designed fluorescence detection steps, a convenient paper-based biosensor for visual detection of multiple catecholamines was formed by pre-dropping the probe on the detection layer, which can be integrated into the portable platform and the detection value can directly display on the smartphone. Therefore, the integrated portable platform of the biosensor can differentiate catecholamines types through fluorescent color and intelligently detect the content of catecholamine in the sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spermine
Multiple catecholamines
Polychromatic carbon nanodots
Readable
Point-of-care testing

Point of care testing (POCT) for multiple catecholamines

PL mechanism of polychromatic carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of multiple CAs in solution system
Point of care testing system for multiple CAs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and the corresponding size distribution of (a) Y-CDs, (b) G-CDs, (c) B-CDs; FT-IR spectra of (d) Y-CDs, (e) G-CDs, (f) B-CDs; UV–vis absorbance (black line), fluorescence excitation (dotted line) and emission (solid line) spectra of g) Y-CDs, h) G-CDs, i) B-CDs.
Possible reaction pathways of Ep, NE and l-DOPA with spermine.
Schematic illustration of possible energy structures and PL emission processes of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs.

Ratiometric fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of (a) Ep; (b) NE and (c) l-DOPA; The linear regression curve plots of fluorescence intensity ratio against the concentration of (d) Ep; (e) NE and (f) l-DOPA. Inset: Corresponding fluorescence photos under the exposure of ultraviolet lamp (365 nm).
(a) Setup of POCT system; (b) Linear regression curve plots for i) Ep, ii) NE and iii) l-DOPA and the corresponding fluorescence images.
Schematic diagram for the synthesis of multicolored polymer carbon dots through spermine-induced catecholamines assembly, and point of care testing device of multiple catecholamines.
The POCT of Ep, NE and l-DOPA in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Spermine induced in-situ synthesis of polychromatic carbon nanodots towards smartphone-readable ratiometric fluorescence sensing of multiple catecholamines

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Abnormal levels of catecholamines can indicate underlying health issues, such as Parkinson's disease, hypertension, and pheochromocytoma. However, it is difficult to accurately differentiate multiple catecholamines using traditional detection methods due to their highly similar structures. Herein, we proposed a carbon-based fluorescent probe with adaptability named spermine that can react with multiple catecholamines to generate polymer carbon dots with distinct fluorescence colors, enabling visual differentiation and quantitative detection of epinephrine, norepinephrine and levodopa. Spermine does not requires synthesis and exhibits excellent biocompatibility, as it naturally exists in bacteria and most animal cells. Additionally, ratiometric fluorescent probes with high sensitivity was constructed to eliminate nonspecific factors in the sample detection process. Furthermore, due to the simplicity of the designed fluorescence detection steps, a convenient paper-based biosensor for visual detection of multiple catecholamines was formed by pre-dropping the probe on the detection layer, which can be integrated into the portable platform and the detection value can directly display on the smartphone. Therefore, the integrated portable platform of the biosensor can differentiate catecholamines types through fluorescent color and intelligently detect the content of catecholamine in the sample.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of multicolored polymer carbon dots through spermine-induced catecholamines assembly, and point of care testing device of multiple catecholamines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Catecholamines are a group of neurotransmitters that play essential roles in the central and peripheral nervous systems. The main catecholamines include epinephrine (Ep), norepinephrine (NE) and dopamine (DA) [1,2]. Abnormal levels of CAs are associated with various neurological and psychiatric disorders such as Alzheimer's disease [3], Parkinson's disease [4], schizophrenia [5], along with other conditions like pheochromocytoma, cardiomyopathy, hypertension, and obesity [6]. However, the types of catecholamines associated with different diseases may vary due to the unique and crucial physiological roles that different CA play in the nervous system [7]. Specifically, Ep, NE and DA regulate cardiovascular function, emotions and stress responses, movement control, as well as attention and wakefulness [8], respectively. Levodopa (l-DOPA), a substance closely related to the levels of catecholamines in vivo, is commonly used to treat symptoms of Parkinson's disease and Parkinson's syndrome as it serves as a precursor to DA. Therefore, differentiating and detecting multiple CAs holds significant importance for the early diagnosis and precise management of diseases, as well as understanding their pathogenesis.

Up to now, the methods for measuring CAs mainly include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [10], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [11], electrochemical [12], fluorescence [13,14] and capillary electrophoresis [15]. Among these, fluorescence detection has advantages over other methods, including high sensitivity, nondestructive, multiplexing, quantification, speed and simplicity, and versatility. As far as we know, existing fluorescence detection strategies primarily employ fluorescence quenching mechanisms for the quantitative detection of individual CA [16–19]. Fluorescence quenching-based detection requires the synthesis of stable and specific fluorescent material as probes, and can only quantitatively detect a single target, which is determined by the single emission of the fluorescent probe. But it is not excluded that a few fluorescent probes with dual emission peaks may exhibit independent response characteristics. However, there are few reports on employing in situ ratiometric fluorescence with on-the-spot detection to achieve visual differentiation and quantitative detection of multiple CAs.

In this work, we proposed a carbon-based fluorescent probe with adaptability for the visual differentiation and quantitative detection of multiple CAs (Scheme 1 ). Spermine directly react with CAs to generate polymer carbon dots with different fluorescent colors, that is, the fluorescence colors resulting from the reactions of spermine with Ep and NE are yellow and green, respectively, while a mixture with DA shows no apparent fluorescence. Interestingly, the reaction between spermine and l-DOPA, the precursor of DA, results in the formation of polymer carbon dots with blue fluorescence, allowing for indirect prediction of DA content. Fluorescence-stable reference materials were introduced to reduces external interferences on the detection. The ratio of fluorescence emission intensities when using fluorescence spectrophotometer or the ratio of red-green-blue channel emission intensities when using a smartphone camera and RGB image processing is used to accurately measure the concentration of CAs. We demonstrate CAs biosensing in human serum and a paper-based assay with smartphone imaging for point-of-care CAs monitoring.

10 μL of Ep, NE and l-DOPA were introduced into a mixed solution consisting of 50 μL of Spermine solution (0.1 M) and 440 μL of pH 9.0 Tris-HCl buffer (0.01 M), respectively. The optimal reaction conditions for the formation of the fluorescent substances, namely Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs, are as follows: 90 °C for 3 min, 80 °C for 10 min and 30 °C for 6 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of multiple catecholamines in solution system
ce:section.ce:para: 10 μL of Ep solution at various concentrations were added into quartz cuvettes containing 430 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (0.01 M, pH = 9.0) and 50 μL of Spermine solution (0.1 M). These cuvettes, each housing a different concentration of Ep, were then subjected to incubation at 90 °C for 3 min. Following incubation, 10 μL of AuNCs (100 mg/mL) were added to each solution. Subsequently, fluorescence emission spectra were acquired using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, employing an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. It is noteworthy that the experimental procedures for NE and l-DOPA mirrored that of Ep, albeit with nuanced adjustments to the experimental parameters. For NE, the temperature during incubation was maintained at 80 °C for 10 min. Similarly, for l-DOPA, the incubation temperature was regulated to 30 °C for 6 min, with the addition of 10 μL of 1 mg/mL G-QDs to serve as an internal fluorescence reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Point of care testing (POCT) for multiple catecholamines
ce:section.ce:para: 60 μL of Ep solution at various concentrations were dropped on the buffer layer of prepared paper-based sensor. The sensor was then carefully placed into an oven at 70 °C for 3 min. Subsequently, 40 μL 5 mg/mL of AuNCs were dropped on the buffer layer of the resulting sensor. 50 μL of NE solution, featuring different concentrations, were introduced onto the buffer layer of the prepared paper-based sensor. The sensor was then subjected to an incubation at 70 °C for 5 min, after which 30 μL of AuNCs solution (5 mg/mL) were applied onto the sensor. Similarly, 50 μL of l-DOPA solution, with varying concentrations, were applied onto the buffer layer of the sensor. Subsequent incubation at 30 °C for 6 min ensured precise reaction kinetics. Finally, 30 μL of G-QDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) were dispensed onto the resulting sensor. Upon completion of the reaction process, fluorescence images of the detection layer were captured using a smartphone under the excitation of ultraviolet lamp (365 nm). These images were then imported into Matlab application software installed on the smartphone for sophisticated RGB analysis and precise determination of target concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and characterization of Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Spermine reacts with Ep, NE and l-DOPA to generate three polymer carbon dots with different fluorescent colors: yellow, green and blue, and are hereafter referred to as Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs. The lack of fluorescence in the mixture of spermine and DA may be attributed to DA's poor solubility in alkaline conditions.

To further investigate the morphology, surface structure and formation process of the multicolored polymer carbon dots generated from the reaction of spermine with CAs, we characterized them using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), mass spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM images (Fig. 1 a–c) revealed that Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs showed well-distributed, whose average sizes were 1.42 ± 0.27, 1.05 ± 0.23 and 1.57 ± 0.37 nm, respectively. Multicolored polymer carbon dots have similar sizes while show different emission peaks, indicating that quantum size effect cannot be the main factor for the red shift of fluorescence emission from B-CDs to Y-CDs [20]. Taking quinine sulfate as standard, the relative quantum yields of Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs are calculated to be 0.75 %, 2.43 % and 4.53 %, respectively.

Compare with Ep, NE and l-DOPA, FT-IR spectra of Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs in Fig. 1d–f appeared two new characteristic peaks located around 1040 cm−1 (the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–N bond) [21] and 1588 cm−1 (the stretching vibration of CN bond) [22], suggesting that spermine may react with CAs via Schiff base reactions. Moreover, using mass spectra to explore the possible chemical structure of the multicolor polymer carbon dots, the peaks at m/z of 344.24, 330.23 and 358.37 in Fig. S1 may be owing to the possible chemical structures of Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs.

XPS was employed to study the elemental composition and surface functional groups of multicolored polymer carbon dots. It is apparent from the full spectra of Figs. S2a–c that they mainly contain three elements: carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O). After peak fitting, the C 1s spectra of Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs possess C–N/C–O (285.3 eV) [23] and CN/CO (287.4 eV) [24] and the N1s spectra possess CN, NH2 and C–N–C [25]. According to their integrated area, the relative contents of different functional groups on the surface of multicolored polymer carbon dots were calculated to provide relative quantitative analysis. As illustrated in Table S1, it can be seen that the fluorescence red-shift was accompanied by the increase of CN content.

To analyze the optical properties, the UV–vis absorption, PL emission and excitation were used to test multicolored polymer carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 1g–i, the maximum emission wavelengths of Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs at the optimal excitation wavelength are 560 nm, 520 nm, and 440 nm, respectively. There are two characteristic absorption peaks in the UV–vis absorption spectra. One absorption peak located at near 240 nm due to the π→π∗ transition of CC in aromatic rings [20], which do not usually generate fluorescence. The other appeared around 300 nm that can be assigned to the n→π∗ transition of CN/CO [26]. It is worth noting that the broad absorption bands around 300 nm of B-CDs, G-CDs and Y-CDs have red-shifted (Fig. S3), that is, the energy levels have narrowed, which is in good agreement with the actually tested bandgap variation (Fig. S4).

As widely recognized, under alkaline conditions, the catechol structure of CAs are prone to oxidation, transforming into unstable o-benzoquinone, followed by spontaneous intermolecular cyclization and rearrangement [27–30]. Spermine is a polyamine substance containing two amino groups and two imino groups, whose amino groups can undergo Schiff base reaction with the carbonyl carbon of o-benzoquinone. The schematic diagram of the possible reaction pathways was presented in Fig. 2 . Specifically, CA is in an alkaline environment constructed by buffer and spermine solution during the reaction, which makes its catechol structure is oxidized and then transformed into unstable o-benzoquinone, accompanied by spontaneous intermolecular cyclization and rearrangement, that is, the structure 1 as shown in Fig. 2 is generated. The structure 2 generated by Schiff base reaction between structure 1 and spermine can rapidly auto-polymerizes to form polymer carbon dots through covalent attachment and aggregation. FT-IR, XPS, and mass spectra tests were conducted on the three polymer carbon dots generated from the reactions of spermine with Ep, NE and l-DOPA, of which the analysis results confirmed the occurrence of the mentioned reaction processes. The CN double bond is one of the important features of Schiff base reactions. The FT-IR spectra of the products show a characteristic absorption peak around 1588 cm⁻1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of CN. And the peak fitting of the XPS data also identify a peak associated with the CN bond. In addition, the molecular weight obtained from mass spectrometry are basically consistent with that of the molecular structure deduced from the possible reaction mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL mechanism of polychromatic carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescence (PL) mechanisms related to polymer carbon dots with different fluorescence emissions was delved into an in-depth exploration. At present, the most widely accepted PL mechanisms are surface states [31], quantum size effects [32], crosslink-enhanced emission [33] and molecule state [34]. According to TEM analysis data, Y-CDs, G-CDs and B-CDs have similar sizes while exhibit different fluorescence emissions, indicating that quantum size effect is not the main factor for the PL of multi-color polymer carbon dots. Typically, as particle size increases, the energy of photons decreases, corresponding to longer wavelengths. However, the fluorescence (FL) mechanism of polymer carbon dots is also influenced by jagged edge sites and defect effects. Therefore, size-independent FL can be observed, attributed to the dominant role of surface state emissions in the FL characteristics. FT-IR and XPS data suggested that the surface of Y-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs have different surface functional groups and different contents of similar functional groups, which leads to different surface state energy levels and emission traps, resulting in different fluorescence emission of three polymer carbon dots [25,35]. Previous studies have shown that the CN functional group on the surface of carbon dots can introduce new energy levels into their electronic structures, thereby enabling carbon dots to exhibit polychromatic emission [36,37]. In this work, XPS analysis results shows that the CN content on the surface of polymer carbon dots increases with the red shift of fluorescence emission, which is consistent with the theoretical conclusion. Fig. 3 illustrates the potential energy structures, PL emission processes and corresponding bandgap widths of the Y-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs to explain their multicolor PL emissions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of multiple CAs in solution system
ce:section.ce:para: The different fluorescence colors of polymer carbon dots generated from the reactions of spermine with Ep, NE and l-DOPA are utilized for visually differentiation multiple CAs, while the concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity is employed for the quantitative detection of CAs. Before carrying out multiple CAs detection experiments, we studied the optimal experimental conditions to ensure that the sensing system is in an optimal state. Guided by the method of controlled variables and judged by the fluorescence intensity, the best experimental parameters including reaction time, reaction temperature, Tris-HCl buffer concentration and pH value for the fluorescence detection of Ep (Fig. S5), NE (Fig. S6) and l-DOPA (Fig. S7) were studied. And various amino acids, glucose, AA, 5-HT and DA were selected as interfering substances for selective experiments to validate the applicability of the proposed probe in practical application (Fig. S8).

Under the optimal experimental conditions, ratiometric fluorescence probes were constructed for multiple CAs. Considering the characteristics of each target under single emission fluorescence detection (Fig. S9), AuNCs and G-QDs with emission peaks of 670 nm and 540 nm were selected as the internal reference. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the concentration of Ep increases, the fluorescence emission peak at 550 nm gradually increases, accompanied by a gradual transition of fluorescence colors from red to yellow. The calibration curve was plotted in Fig. 4d, manifesting the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio and Ep concentration within the concentration range of 0–0.01 mM, with the specific relationship equation of F
550
/F
670 = 65.15X
Ep +0.21 (R2 = 0.999) (F, fluorescence intensity in a. u.; X, target concentration in mM). For NE (Fig. 4b), in the concentration of 0–0.025 mM, the fluorescence intensity ratio with different concentrations of NE had good linear relationship in Fig. 4e. The corresponding linear equations of F
520
/F
670 toward NE concentration was F
520
/F
670 = 138.16X
NE +0.34 (R2 = 0.999). Unlike Ep and NE, the internal reference of l-DOPA was G-QDs, as excitation at the optimal excitation wavelength (340 nm) of l-DOPA can result in incomplete fluorescence spectra of AuNCs. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, increasing the concentration of l-DOPA made the fluorescence color of the solution change from green to blue. The calibration curve in Fig. 4f explained that the fluorescence intensity ratio F
440
/F
540 had good linear relationship with concentrations of l-DOPA, that is, F
440
/F
540 = 392.23 X
L-DOPA +0.86 (R2 = 0.998). Their lowest detection limits (LODs) of Ep, NE and l-DOPA was determined as 22.6 nM, 14.4 nM and 3.82 nM based on 3 N/S rule (N, the standard variance of blank groups with the buffer solution instead of the target; S, slope of the linear expression). Fig. S10 shows the fluorescence intensity ratio curves of Ep, NE and l-DOPA with different concentrations after participating in the reaction. The recovery experiment of multiple CAs in human serum has been conducted, with specific data available in Table S2. In addition, we also calculated the LODs under single emission fluorescence detection, as detailed in Table S3, to confirm the improvement of sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescence detection. At the same time, Table S4 lists the detection results of different detection methods for detecting Ep, NE, and l-DOPA, which suggests that the detection strategy proposed in this study is effective in improving the reliability and accuracy of detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Point of care testing system for multiple CAs
ce:section.ce:para: After investigation of the fluorescence sensing behavior and mechanism of probe, we integrated smartphone with the microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD) loaded with spermine to form a POCT device for multiple CAs detection. Using 3D origami technology, the μPAD with three layers was developed, featuring a buffer layer with a water-soluble polymer solution for the even dispersion of CAs, a detection layer loaded with spermine and the isolation layer for protecting. Details on the fabrication of spermine-μPAD are provided in Fig. S11. In this work, the fluorescence images involved were taken by smartphone (Apple iPhone 14 pro), with each image having a resolution of 3024 × 4032 px. Fig. S12 depicts the rich fluorescence color changes in the detection of different concentrations of CAs on spermine-μPAD, laying the foundation for intelligent real-time detection. In addition, the smartphone embedded with Matlab programs (see supporting information for details) are used to capture fluorescent images, realize the analysis of RGB values of fluorescence images and the calculation of target concentration.

The schematic diagram for POCT of multiple CAs is shown in Fig. 5 a. Specifically, an appropriate amount of CA solution of unknown concentration is dropped onto the pre-fabricated paper-based microfluidic chip. After heating to the suitable temperature, the prepared reference solution is added. And then the chip is placed in the installed detection box. The Matlab software storing the RGB analysis program and UV lamp (365 nm) are turned on to capture the fluorescence image. Subsequently, the RGB value of the captured image is analyzed to determine the concentration of the CA.

The detection performance was evaluated to monitor the fluorescence response of designed intelligent analytical device towards de-ionized water spiked with different concentrations of multiple CAs. It was found that (G-B)/R was to be good linear relationship (R2 = 0.98) with Ep concentration across the range of 0.001–0.1 mM, given by (G-B)/R = 2.25X
Ep -0.17 (Fig. 5b(i)). For NE, as shown in Fig. 5b(ii), it had a negative linear correlation between (R-G)/B and concentration with a correlation equation of (R-G)/B = −6.99X
NE +0.6 (R2 = 0.97) across the concentration range of 0–0.1 mM. Similarly, there was a good linear relationship between the (R-G)/B and l-DOPA concentration in the concentration range of 0–0.5 mM, that is, (R-G)/B = 0.95X
L-DOPA -0.58 with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. 5b(iii)). The LODs of Ep, NE and l-DOPA was 7.2, 2.6 and 19.1 μM, respectively. As shown in Figs. S13a–c, the designed μPAD was capable of giving a fluorescence response to multiple CAs samples upon storage for up to 1 month at dry, light-protected, and sealed low-temperature with an average coefficient of variance lower than 6.5 %, suggesting that the analytical device has good stability for a long time. Moreover, based on the results of several experiments, we made standard cards (Fig. S14) for determining the approximate concentration of CAs by colorimetry.

The feasibility of designed intelligent analytical device for detection of multiple CAs in human serum samples were then verified. The recoveries of Ep, NE and l-DOPA were evaluated, yielding a range of 96.9–102.8 %, and the corresponding RSD were 1.19–6.65 %, as detailed in Table 1 . The satisfactory recovery and relative standard deviation suggested that the intelligent analytical device is highly feasible for the detection of Ep, NE and l-DOPA in real samples.

In summary, we proposed an in-situ carbon-based fluorescent probe with adaptability that can be integrated with intelligent paper-based analytical devices for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of multiple CAs in human serum. Spermine can react with Ep, NE and l-DOPA to generate polymer carbon dots with three different fluorescent colors, enabling visual differentiation and quantitative detection of multiple CAs. Introducing fluorescence-stable internal reference material to construct ratio fluorescence probes, the robustness and sensitivity of CAs detection has been enhanced. Moreover, a portable, low-cost paper-based analytical device loaded with spermine is integrated with a smartphone to form a POCT device for multiple CAs. The introduction of innovative in-situ carbon-based fluorescent probe with adaptability and the construction of POCT device with readable detection results provide the possibility for visual differentiation and sensitive, portable detection of multiple CAs.

Jiajing Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jia An: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yaqin Han: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Junan Fang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Yufei Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2021jcyj-jqX0014), and Chongqing Entrepreneurship and Innovation Support Program (CX201803), and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No.2021YFB2800203). Thanks to the Analysis and Testing Center of Chongqing University and Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for providing the test platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.127292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectroscopic methods for determination of catecholamines: a mini-review
ApSRv

Coord. Chem. Rev.
